title: Tomato Harvest Pricing Run Seed 42-2
scenario: Tomato Ketchup in Ontario. Supplier-to-manufacturer pricing sets the manufacturer cost basis before the retailer negotiation.

product_context.demand_signal: Retail ketchup demand is steady, but wholesale buyers want price protection before peak tomato procurement.
product_context.supply_signal: Tomato harvest yields are uneven across growing regions, putting pressure on paste input pricing.

agents.name: Supplier
agents.objective: Sell above the supplier reservation price while keeping volume committed.

agents.name: Manufacturer
agents.objective: Buy below the procurement ceiling and preserve downstream margin.

agents.name: Retailer
agents.objective: Buy enough units without exceeding the retail reservation price.

phases.name: supplier_manufacturer
phases.label: Supplier to Manufacturer
phases.description: The supplier negotiates an upstream unit price with the manufacturer.

phases.name: manufacturer_retailer
phases.label: Manufacturer to Retailer
phases.description: The manufacturer negotiates a downstream unit price with the retailer after the upstream deal sets its cost basis.

steps.message: Thanks for opening the discussion. Based on current market conditions, I can offer supply at 6.70 per unit for this round, which is in line with the latest pricing environment and gives you reliable committed volume. If you have a larger volume target or specific term commitments in mind, I may be able to work with you on structure, but I’d like to start from a level that reflects the market properly. No buyer offer has been received yet, so the reasonable move is to anchor the negotiation with an initial seller proposal near the observed market reference of 6.38 while leaving room to negotiate.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

tool_calls.result_summary: Reviewed state for supplier_manufacturer round 1. Pending message: none.
Observed market reference price: 6.38.

steps.message: Thanks for the opening proposal. That level is well above where this purchase can work for us on a manufacturing input basis, so to move the conversation forward I can open at 2.22 per unit. If you have flexibility tied to volume, scheduling, or term length, I’m willing to discuss structure, but we would need to stay much closer to this range than to 6.70. The supplier anchored very high relative to our buying economics, so a low counter is the most reasonable way to signal the gap and test their flexibility. It is too early to walk away, and making a counteroffer preserves room to negotiate while protecting downstream margin.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.22, below its hidden seller floor of 2.83.

delivered_message.note: Thanks for opening the discussion. Based on current market conditions, I can offer supply at 6.70 per unit for this round, which is in line with the latest pricing environment and gives you reliable committed volume. If you have a larger volume target or specific term commitments in mind, I may be able to work with you on structure, but I’d like to start from a level that reflects the market properly. No buyer offer has been received yet, so the reasonable move is to anchor the negotiation with an initial seller proposal near the observed market reference of 6.38 while leaving room to negotiate.

Observed market reference price: 7.45.

steps.message: I appreciate the counter, but 2.22 is far below a workable supply level for us and doesn’t reflect current market conditions or the commitment required on our side. To keep this moving, I can revise from 6.70 to 6.10 per unit as a good-faith step, especially if we can pair it with meaningful volume and a reliable schedule. If you can come up materially from your opening position, there may be room to shape terms around quantity or duration. The buyer’s offer is at the supplier’s floor and far below the latest market reference of 11.10, so accepting would leave too much value on the table. A moderate concession is reasonable to test seriousness while maintaining a strong anchor and preserving room to negotiate on volume.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: Thanks for the movement, but 6.10 is still well outside a range we can make work on our side, even with the current market reference in mind. We do have room to improve from our opening position, and I can move to 2.38 per unit if we can align on meaningful volume and a dependable release schedule. If you have flexibility tied to commitment or contract length, bring your number down materially and we can keep narrowing the gap. The supplier remains far above a workable buy level, so accepting is not reasonable. A modest increase from 2.22 to 2.38 signals seriousness without giving up too much margin and keeps the negotiation alive.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.38, below its hidden seller floor of 2.83.

steps.message: I appreciate the movement, but 2.38 per unit is still far below a level we can responsibly commit to, especially against the current market reference. To keep this moving, I can make a substantial concession from 6.10 and come down to 5.20 per unit if we can pair it with meaningful volume and a reliable release schedule. If you can improve materially from your side, we should still have room to narrow this further. The buyer’s offer is well above our floor but still extremely far from prevailing market context and our current ask. A counteroffer keeps the negotiation alive, signals flexibility, and uses the strong market reference to justify holding a much higher price while still making a visible concession.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: Thanks for the movement from 6.10 to 5.20. That said, we are still nowhere near a level that works for our production economics and downstream commitments, even if we assume stable volume and scheduling on our side. To keep this alive, I can improve modestly to 2.46 per unit today, but any further movement from us would need to be matched by a much more substantial concession from your side. The supplier made a concession but remains far above a workable buy level for the manufacturer. A small increase signals engagement without giving up too much margin, and keeps the negotiation open rather than ending it prematurely.

steps.message: I appreciate the signal that you want to keep this moving, but 2.46 is still far below a level we can justify given current market conditions and our production commitments. We have already moved materially from 6.10 to 5.20, so any further concession has to be measured rather than dramatic. I can come down to 4.55 per unit if we are working toward a real close, and I would need you to narrow the gap much more meaningfully from your side on the next move. The buyer's offer remains well below a reasonable selling range from the supplier's perspective, especially relative to the latest market reference of 10.40. A counteroffer keeps the negotiation alive, shows flexibility, and pressures the buyer to make a more substantial concession without ending talks prematurely.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: Thanks for moving, but 4.55 is still far beyond a level that works for our economics. We are already stretching to stay engaged here, and based on our downstream margin constraints I can increase to 2.54 per unit as a best next step. If you want to get this done in the remaining turns, we need a much sharper move from your side toward a practical closing range. Their offer remains dramatically above our workable buy level, so accepting would not be sensible. Rejecting outright would end the conversation too early, and a firm but modest increase lets us show movement while signaling that our room is very limited.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.54, below its hidden seller floor of 2.83.

steps.message: I appreciate the movement to 2.54, but that level is still well below a workable supply price for us, especially given current market indications and the need to protect committed volume. To keep this alive in the final turn, I can make a meaningful concession and come down to 4.10 per unit. That reflects real movement from our side, and if you're serious about closing, I need to see a substantial step up from your last number. The buyer is far below even a plausible closing range, but there is still one turn left and the market reference is very strong relative to both offers. A further concession keeps the negotiation open without giving away too much leverage, while signaling urgency and a credible final-zone price.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: I see you've moved to 4.10, but that is still far outside a range I can responsibly take on and preserve a workable downstream position. Given where we are at the end of the negotiation, I'll make one final move to 2.72 per unit as a serious effort to close the gap. That's above my prior number and reflects real stretch on my side, but it would need to be the basis for an immediate agreement. Their latest offer remains well above my workable buy economics, so accepting would damage margin. With the market reference strong, they have leverage, but making a modest final concession is more reasonable than walking away immediately.

negotiations.label: Supplier to Manufacturer
negotiations.outcome_summary: Supplier to Manufacturer reached the turn limit without agreement.

diagnosis.outcome: Upstream deal timeout. Downstream negotiation did not run because the upstream deal did not close.
diagnosis.chain_effect: The upstream deal did not establish a downstream cost basis, so phase two was skipped.
key_risks: A costly upstream agreement compresses downstream negotiating room.
Turn limits can force timeouts even when the gap is narrowing.
suggested_next_actions: Tune reservation prices and turn limits to test sensitivity.
Add richer quantity or margin tradeoffs before introducing LLM behavior.

notes: Backend simulation with linked upstream and downstream negotiations.
